title: Interview Scenario Puzzles
subtitle: Multi-step quoting under game pressure
intro: Prop-trading interviews often run live market-making games: a moderator describes a payoff, traders shout quotes, and information arrives in stages. Success combines fast fair-value math, disciplined two-sided quotes, inventory awareness, and calm updates when the picture changes.

Classic setups include rolling dice for sums or products, drawing cards with rank-based payoffs, and flipping multiple coins where results are revealed one at a time. None of these require memorizing proprietary materials — just probability, expected value, and good quoting habits.

This capstone lesson mixes integrated scenarios: compute fair, choose spread, react to clues, and recognize when a counterparty's trade tells you something about your quote. Treat each question like a compressed game round.
Finish this track and you are ready to practice in Learn Mode and Play Mode with the same vocabulary and instincts.

Capstone rounds compress everything: compute fair from a verbal spec, choose spread width given volatility and your confidence, update after public clues, and skew when fills pile on one side. Interviewers watch whether you speak your reasoning aloud while adjusting — silence with a wrong quote is worse than a slow correct one.

Treat each scenario as a live game tick: state fair, state bid and ask, and when the moderator announces news, pause fills mentally, recompute conditional fair, then replace your quote entirely. The winners are rarely the fastest mental calculators alone — they are the ones who keep quotes coherent under pressure.

primer.heading: The market-making workflow
body: Every live game round follows the same loop: **compute fair value**, **choose a spread**, then **state a two-sided quote** centered on fair. Say it out loud and commit.
This order never changes. Skipping straight to a number — quoting before you have fair — is how candidates get picked off in the first ten seconds.

keyTerms.definition: The expected payoff; the center of your quote.
A bid and an ask stated together, e.g. `6 at 8`.

visual.caption: The loop, every round
items: **Update** when fair, inventory, or news changes

primer.heading: Computing fair fast
body: Interview payoffs reduce to a few patterns. Sums use linearity (two dice = `7`); independent products multiply (`3.5 × 3.5 = 12.25`); dollar payoffs are probability times prize (`$10` on red = `$5`).
Recognize the pattern, plug in the means, and you have fair before anyone else has finished enumerating.

example.prompt: Contract pays the product of two fair dice. What is fair value?

primer.heading: Choose a spread, center on fair
body: With fair in hand, pick a width and place the bid and ask symmetrically. Fair `12` with spread `2` is `11 at 13`.
Tighten when confident, widen when the payoff is volatile or a reveal is coming. Center matters more than width — a tight quote in the wrong place is a gift.

example.prompt: Fair is `12`; spread policy is `2`, centered. What is your quote?

visual.expression: bid = fair − spread/2,   ask = fair + spread/2
visual.caption: Symmetric quote around fair value.

primer.heading: Flow is a signal
body: Who trades against you carries information. If aggressive traders keep **lifting your ask**, your offer is probably below fair — raise your quotes. If they keep **hitting your bid**, your bid is too rich — lower them.
One fill is noise; repeated one-sided flow is a message. React without panicking.

keyTerms.definition: The pattern of who buys and sells against you; a clue about where fair really is.

primer.heading: Update on news; manage inventory
body: When the moderator reveals a clue, recompute the **conditional fair** and replace your quote — a stale quote is the easiest pick-off in the game.
Meanwhile watch your position. Fills pile up on one side, so **skew** toward getting flat, and near the bell prioritize flattening even if you give up a little edge.

keyTerms.definition: Fair value recomputed after a clue, e.g. one die shown = `d + 3.5`.
Reduce inventory toward zero to cut end-of-game risk.

example.prompt: You quoted `6 at 8` on a two-dice sum (fair `7`). A die is revealed as `3`. Re-quote?

visual.title: Coherence beats speed
visual.text: Speak your reasoning, re-quote on every change, and keep bid below ask. A slightly slow correct quote beats a fast incoherent one.

primerNarration.title: Fair, spread, quote
primerNarration.caption: Every live round follows the same loop. Compute fair value, choose a spread, then state a two-sided quote centered on fair. Say it out loud and commit. The order never changes; quoting before you have fair is how you get picked off in the first ten seconds.

items: Choose a spread for your uncertainty
Quote bid and ask around fair

primerNarration.title: Recognize the pattern
primerNarration.caption: Interview payoffs reduce to a few patterns. Sums add their means, so two dice are seven. Independent products multiply, so two dice are twelve and a quarter. Dollar payoffs are probability times prize, so ten dollars on red is five. Recognize the pattern and plug in.

primerNarration.title: Pick a width, place it
primerNarration.caption: With fair in hand, pick a width and place the bid and ask symmetrically. Fair twelve with a spread of two is eleven at thirteen. Tighten when confident, widen before a volatile reveal. Center matters more than width: a tight quote in the wrong place is a gift.

visual.expression: bid = fair − spread/2,  ask = fair + spread/2

primerNarration.title: Read the room
primerNarration.caption: Who trades against you carries information. If traders keep lifting your ask, your offer is probably below fair, so raise your quotes. If they keep hitting your bid, your bid is too rich, so lower them. One fill is noise, but repeated one-sided flow is a message.

primerNarration.title: News reprices, position pulls
primerNarration.caption: When the moderator reveals a clue, recompute the conditional fair and replace your quote, because a stale quote is the easiest pick-off in the game. Meanwhile watch your position. Skew toward getting flat, and near the bell prioritize flattening even if you give up a little edge.

visual.text: Re-quote on every change, keep bid below ask, and flatten before the bell.

questions.question: Two fair dice rolled. Contract pays the product of the two faces (1–6 each). Fair value is closest to:

explanations.A: Seven is the expected sum, not product.
explanations.D: Thirty-six is max product (6×6), not average.

remediation.question: E[X×Y] for independent X,Y equals:
Single die E[face] = 3.5. Product of two independent dice EV ≈

questions.question: Fair value computed as 12. Spread policy: 2 wide, centered on fair. Best integer quote:

explanations.D: Spread 1 and bid nearly equals ask incorrectly.

remediation.question: Fair 20, spread 4, integer centered quote:
After computing fair, next step before shouting a quote:
options: Choose spread and center bid/ask on fair

questions.question: Draw one card from 52. Pay $10 if red, $0 if black. Fair value:

explanations.C: Ten is max red payout, not average.
explanations.D: Twenty-six is count of red cards, not dollars.

remediation.question: P(red) in standard deck:
$20 if red else $0. Fair value:

questions.question: You quote 14 at 16 on a dice-sum game. An aggressive trader immediately lifts your 16. Most likely interpretation:
options: They think fair value is above 16
They think fair value is below 14

explanations.A: Correct. Buying at 16 suggests they value the contract above your ask.
explanations.B: Below 14 they'd sell to your bid.
explanations.D: Wide spread doesn't explain eager lift.

remediation.question: Repeated lifts of your ask suggest:
options: Your ask may be below fair — consider raising quotes

explanations.A: Correct — buyers see value above your offer.

remediation.question: Someone hits your bid repeatedly. Likely:

explanations.A: Correct — sellers think your bid is rich.

questions.question: Three fair coins (0/1). Pay sum. Coin 1 = 1, Coin 2 = 1, Coin 3 still hidden. Fair value:

explanations.D: Three is max if last coin were 1 for sure.

remediation.question: Two coins show 1, one hidden. Expected sum:
Each hidden fair 0/1 coin adds to EV:

questions.question: Opening round of a mock trading game. Unknown payoff described. Your FIRST step should be:
options: Shout a tight quote immediately without calculating
Compute fair value, then quote a reasonable spread centered near fair
Only watch others and never quote

explanations.B: Correct. Fair → spread → two-sided quote is the standard workflow.

remediation.question: Disciplined market makers typically:
options: Update quotes when fair or inventory changes

remediation.question: Spread earns edge; fair value centers quote to avoid:

explanations.A: Correct — mis-centering loses to informed flow.
explanations.B: You want fair trading at fair+spread.

questions.question: Game: sum of two fair dice, fair = 7. You are flat. Uncertainty is moderate. Best initial two-sided quote?

explanations.A: Correct. Symmetric spread 2 around fair 7 is the textbook opening quote in mock games.
explanations.B: Zero spread earns no compensation and is atypical in practice rounds.
explanations.C: Off-center low invites sellers to hit a bid above their updated view after clues.
explanations.D: Off-center high skews long unnecessarily when flat.

remediation.question: Fair 7, flat. Midpoint of 6 at 8 is:
Flat inventory → quote should be:

questions.question: You quoted 6 at 8 on two-dice sum (fair 7). Moderator reveals first die = 3. New fair ≈ 6.5. You are still flat. Best immediate re-quote?

explanations.A: Correct. 3 + 3.5 = 6.5; symmetric spread around updated fair.
explanations.B: Old quote centered at 7 is stale — ask 8 is rich vs 6.5 fair, bid 6 is cheap; both wrong directionally.
explanations.D: 3 at 4 ignores expected sum, uses single die incorrectly.

remediation.question: Die shows 3. Expected sum:
After news, first step:

questions.question: Fair 50. You are long 3 (limit +4) after getting hit on your bid several times. Your last quote was 48 at 52. Best next quote?
options: 47 at 49 — skew down, tight ask to sell

explanations.A: Correct. Long near limit → discourage more lifts, invite selling inventory via relatively attractive ask while shading down.
explanations.B: Symmetric widen doesn't address directional inventory.
explanations.C: Unchanged keeps inviting lifts — you are already long.
explanations.D: Raising ask makes it harder to flatten.

remediation.question: Long near limit. Priority:
Sellers hit your bid repeatedly → you are:

explanations.A: Correct — each hit on your bid is a buy that adds long inventory.
explanations.B: Hits on bid add long, not short.

questions.question: Draw one card: payoff $10 if heart, $0 otherwise. Before draw, fair = 2.5. The card is revealed red (suit known, rank not). New fair = 5. You still quote 2 at 3 from before the reveal. Flat. Best action?
options: Re-quote near 4.5 at 5.5 — old quote is wildly stale low
Keep 2 at 3 — spread width still works
Only tighten to 2.4 at 2.6 around old fair

explanations.A: Correct. Given red, P(heart) = 1/2 so fair = 5; your ask at 3 invites every buyer in the room to lift a cheap offer.
explanations.B: Spread width is meaningless if the center is wrong by two points — you would be picked off instantly.
explanations.C: 0 at 10 is off-center and undisciplined; re-center near 5 first, then choose width.
explanations.D: Tightening around 2.5 ignores the public red-card clue that doubled fair value.

remediation.question: Red card shown. P(heart | red) =

explanations.A: Correct — half of red cards are hearts.

remediation.question: Fair jumps from 2.5 to 5. Your old ask 3 is:

explanations.A: Correct — ask far below new fair.

questions.question: Sum of two dice game, fair 7. You quote 6 at 8 and are flat. A buyer lifts your ask (you sell 1 at 8). The moderator then reveals one die = 5, so new fair = 8.5. You are now short 1. What is your mark-to-market P&L on the position, and what should you do?
options: −$0.50; you are short into a higher fair — raise your quote and look to buy back
+$0.50; you sold high, so you are ahead
$0; the sale locked in edge regardless of the reveal
+$1.00; your ask 8 still beats the old fair 7

explanations.A: Correct. Short 1 sold at 8, new fair 8.5 → (8 − 8.5) × 1 = −$0.50. Fair rose against your short, so raise your quotes and buy to cover.
explanations.B: +$0.50 reverses the sign: you sold at 8 but fair is now 8.5, so the short is underwater, not ahead.
explanations.C: $0 ignores that the reveal moved fair to 8.5 after your sale; the open short must be re-marked to the new fair.
explanations.D: +$1.00 marks against the stale old fair 7 (8 − 7); you must mark to the updated fair of 8.5.

remediation.question: You are short 1 unit, sold at 8. Fair value is now 8.5. Mark-to-market P&L?

explanations.A: Correct — for a short, (sale price − fair) × qty = (8 − 8.5) × 1 = −$0.50.
explanations.B: +$0.50 flips the sign; fair above your sale price loses money on a short.
explanations.C: $8.00 is your sale price, not the P&L.
explanations.D: $8.50 is the current fair, not the P&L.

remediation.question: One die shows 5, the other is hidden. Expected sum of two dice?

explanations.B: 7 is the unconditional two-dice mean, ignoring the reveal.
explanations.C: 5 is just the known die, not the sum.
explanations.D: 11 would require the hidden die to be 6 for certain.
